planting.note: Plant bulbs 6 inches deep and 12 inches apart in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light partial shade in early spring; tiger lily multiplies rapidly from the characteristic black bulbils that form along the leaf axils in summer and can be planted immediately for new plants.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 inches deep and 12 inches apart in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light partial shade in early spring; tiger lily multiplies rapidly from the characteristic black bulbils that form along the leaf axils in summer and can be planted immediately for new plants. Lilium lancifolium is the showiest, most vigorous, and most carefree of all garden lilies, producing 4-6 foot stems in midsummer with up to 20 pendant, strongly reflexed flowers of vivid orange with black spots and recurved petals that are among the most dramatic mid-summer ornamentals in any garden; individual plants bloom for 2-3 weeks and clumps increase rapidly. This lily can carry Lily Symptomless Virus without showing symptoms and can transmit it to other lily species via aphids — plant it away from other Lilium species, especially the more susceptible Asiatic and Oriental hybrids.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 6 inches deep in early spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; protect the emerging shoots in spring with a light covering of straw or fleece in zones 3-5 as they emerge early and are susceptible to late frost damage.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 inches deep in early spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; protect the emerging shoots in spring with a light covering of straw or fleece in zones 3-5 as they emerge early and are susceptible to late frost damage. Lilium regale was introduced from western China to Western horticulture by E.H. Wilson in 1903 and has remained one of the great garden bulbs ever since; it produces large, 6-inch, outward-facing to slightly nodding trumpet flowers with pure white interiors marked with yellow at the center, flushed with wine-purple on the exterior of the petals, and an intoxicatingly powerful fragrance that is unmistakable and carries across the garden on warm evenings. It blooms in midsummer for 2-3 weeks, is easy to grow, and is one of the few lily species that comes reasonably true from seed and flowers in its second or third year; it is excellent cut flower material as well.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall 4-6 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade — martagon lily is one of very few true lilies that genuinely thrives in shade and is the best choice for naturalizing under deciduous trees.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall 4-6 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade — martagon lily is one of very few true lilies that genuinely thrives in shade and is the best choice for naturalizing under deciduous trees. Lilium martagon is a native of European and Asian mountain woodlands, producing slender, 3-5 foot stems whorled with dark-green leaves and large clusters of 10-50 small, nodding flowers with dramatically reflexed (swept-back) petals in shades of pink-purple to dark rose, with dark spotting, in June-July; the flowers are unpleasantly scented but visually striking, and the variety 'Album' produces elegant pure-white flowers. Once planted, martagon lily strongly resents disturbance and should be left undisturbed in its permanent position where it will slowly increase over decades, self-seeding into a beautiful long-lived colony beneath trees; it is essentially maintenance-free once established.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4 inches deep in fall in dry to moist, well-drained, slightly acidic sandy or loamy soil in light partial shade to full sun; Carolina lily is native to open pine woods and sandy woodland edges and performs best with excellent drainage and some protection from the hottest afternoon sun in zone 8-9.

directions: Plant bulbs 4 inches deep in fall in dry to moist, well-drained, slightly acidic sandy or loamy soil in light partial shade to full sun; Carolina lily is native to open pine woods and sandy woodland edges and performs best with excellent drainage and some protection from the hottest afternoon sun in zone 8-9. Lilium michauxi is the southeastern native counterpart to the better-known Turk's cap lily (L. superbum), producing 2-4 foot stems bearing one to several pendant, strongly reflexed orange flowers with prominent red spots and recurved petals from June through July; the flowers are visited by large native bees and sphinx moths and the plant grows naturally in the sandy, acidic soils of longleaf pine flatwoods and open upland woods throughout the Southeast. Unlike many lilies, it is adapted to summer heat and humidity and tolerates zone 8-9 conditions; plant it in a well-drained, partially shaded spot in a native woodland garden where it will slowly increase by bulb offsets.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 8-10 inches deep (the stem roots above the bulb, so depth is important) in moist, well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in partial shade; Henry's lily is unusual among true lilies in tolerating lime-rich soils and partial shade, making it useful in dappled light under trees where other lilies fail.

directions: Plant bulbs 8-10 inches deep (the stem roots above the bulb, so depth is important) in moist, well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in partial shade; Henry's lily is unusual among true lilies in tolerating lime-rich soils and partial shade, making it useful in dappled light under trees where other lilies fail. Lilium henryi was discovered in central China by Augustine Henry in 1888 and introduced to Western horticulture shortly after; it is a spectacular architectural lily, producing stout stems up to 8 feet tall in full vigor bearing large whorls of leaves and 20-30 pendant, Turk's-cap-type flowers of warm apricot-orange with dark spotting and reflexed petals in late July and August. The plant is vigorous, long-lived, disease-resistant, and increases slowly by bulb offsets; its unusual tolerance of chalk and alkaline soils makes it an outstanding choice for gardens on limestone where most lilies are difficult.
